Fnrn Polrrrcel PRACTTcEs CovturssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA g5gl4_232g
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
February 17,2009
John Brown
Best Best & Krieger
One CorporutePlaza
3500 Porsche Way, Suite 200
Ontario, CA91764
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our file No. A-09-278
Dear Mr. Brown:
This letter res-ponds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' rnii letteiis based on the facts presented;
the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when
it renders assistance. (In re Ogtesby (1975) I FppC Ops. 71.)
Additionally, our advice is limited to obligations arising under the Act. We do not address the
applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws sich as common law conflict of interest or
Govemment Code Section 1090.
QUESTTON
Even though the Directors of the Hi-Desert Water District have disqualifying conflicts of
interest, are any of them able to participate in the governmental decisions described below based
on the "Public Generally" or "Legally Required" exceptions to the prohibition?
CONCLUSION
The "public generally" and "legally required" exceptions do appear to apply to the
Directors' situations and would allow each to vote, despitca disqualifying confict of interest.
I The Political Reform Act is contained in covernment code sections g I 000 through g I 0 I 4. All statutory
references are to the Government code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair political practices
commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through I 8997 of Title 2 of thi California Code of Regularions. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of Regutations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-09-278
Page No. 2
You are General Counsel for the Hi-Desert Water District (the "District") and you
request advice on behalf of elected Directors Sheldon Hough, Sarann Graham, Roger irlayer, and
Dan Munsey (collectively, the "Directors"). The DirectorJwill be participating in decisions
related to a proposed construction of a regional wastewater collection, treatment, and disposal
system (the "Project") that will be on-going-and accomplished in three phases. The project will
serve residential, commercial, and industrial customers and will aim to accomplish the iollowing:
l) reduce and/or replace the need for existing sewer septic systems, 2) improvl the collection and
treatment of wastewater, and 3) reduce the amount of nitrate contamination affecting the Warren
Valley Groundwater Basin.
The District will attempt to cover costs through bonds, grants, low-interest loans or other
alternatives. There will likely be costs to the public,-however, i-ncluding contributions by
property owners with improved real property in the District, future coniection fees, and
contributions through existing ratepayers in the District. The rate structure is currently under
review.
The first phase will involve constructing a wastewater reclamation facility designed for
an initial one million gallons per day treatmentiapacity, developing a sewage collection system
to^ serve the developed area of Yucca valley, consiructing percolation basins for the treated
effluent to return the effluent to the WarrenValley Grouid'water Basin. Approximately 50
percent of the District's water customers will be aile to connect to the nroiect at the cornpletion
of the first phase.
, , 
You have stated that each director has the economic interests in the District described
Delow:
FACTS
Hough:
o 
Business entity: dentistry practice.
o 
Real property: building/property for dentistry practice.
o 
source of income: dentistry patients (to the extent that he received $500 or
more in the last 12 months of any one patient).
o 
Personal finances: a public official always has an interest in his or her
personal finances.
Graham
Real property: personal residence and second lot on the adjoining property
containing a garage.
o 
Personal finances: a public official always has an interest in his or her
personal finances.
Mayes
a
Real property: personal residence, commercial lot.


File No. A-09-278
Page No. 3
o 
Source of income: rent income from businesses that lease space on the
commercial lot.
o 
Personal finances: a public official always has an interest in his or her
personal finances.
Munsey
o 
Business entity: Real estate business.
o 
Source of income: Regulation 1g703.3(c)(2)(c) defines the sources of
income to a real estate agent as the brokerage for whom the agent works, the
client represented by qe agent and any person who receives a finder,s or referral
fee for referring the sale to the agent. A person who purchases or sells the real
property from which the agent receives his or her feeis not a source of income to
the agent unless it is the agent,s client.
o 
Personal finances: a public official always has an interest in his or her
personal finances.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 grohillts any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influenc" u !or"*-ental decision in which the
official has a financial interest._ A public official has a "fiiancial interest,, in a governmental
decision' within the meaning of the Act,^if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will have a material financial effect on one or -ir" of the public oifi"iut,, economic
interests' (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The commission has adopted an eighrstep
standard analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifyirg .orr-ni"t of interest in a
given govemmental decision, which we apply to your question. (Regulation 1g700(bxl)-(g).)
Steps One and Two: Are the Directors Public Officials and Are They Making,
Participating in Making, or rnfluencing Governmental Decisions?
The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act only apply to public officials. (Section
87100') Public officials include members of committ"er, bo*ds or commissions with decision-
y*iry authority' (Regulation 18701(aXl).) officials oi all special purpor" Jirtricrs are also
included under the provisions of the rule. As a member of the Hi-Desert water District, the
Directors are public officials under the Act.
A public official'makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
purview of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her agency
to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.1.) You have stated that the directois will be voting on the project, and are
thus making govemmental decisions.


File No. A-09-278
Page No.4
Step Three: What Are the Directors' Applicable Economic Interests?
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest', in a governmental
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any of the ofhcial's1.onorni" interests. The applicable economic
interests include:
l' An interest in a business entity in which a public official has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section g7103(a), Regulation
18703.1(a).) An interest in any business entity in which a'puutic official is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, oi hold, any position of
management. (Secrion 87 I 03(d), Regulation I g703. I (b).)
2' An interest in real property in which a public official'has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section g7l0i(b), Regulation rg7o3.2.)
3. Any source of income, including promised incine, to the public official that
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 103(c), Regulation I 8703.3.)
a'. ftv ryurce of gifts to the publii official if the gifts aggregate to $420or more
within l-2-mo11hs prior to the decision. (Section s-zrortli-negularion 1g703.4.)
5. A public official arso has an economic interest i" hd;;h;;i;r"*i"'"" -
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate
family. This is also known_as the "personal financial effects,, rule. (Section
87103, Regulation I 8703.5.)
As detailed above, the Directors have interests in their income, businesses, and real
properties, as well as their personal finances.
To recap,'^:_:j::.1ough has economic interests in his business enrity (dentistry
practice), re{ property'1the property on which the dentistry practice sits), his patients as sources
of income, if he received more ttran $SOO in the past 12 ;;;fi, fro_ ury orr" futi"nt, and his
personal finances.
Director Graham has an economic interest in her personal residence, a double lot with the
second lot containing a garage as well as her personar finamces.
Director Mayes has an economic interest in the real property on which his personal
residence sits as well as a commercial lot he owns. He has u ,our." of income economic interest
in the rent payments he receives from the businesses that lease space on that commercial lot. He
also has an interest in his personal finances.
Director Munsey has an economic interest in his rear
It is unclear what his role is in this business, whether he also
estate business as a business entity.
acts as an agent, and whether this is
2 A public official has an interest in his or her real property, provided that interest is worth $2,000 or more.
We assume in all instances above that this is the case.


File No. A-09-278
Page No. 5
a brokerage business. The source of income to a real estate agent is the brokerage for whom the
agent works, the client he or she represents, and any person who receives a finder,s fee for
referring the sale.
The Directors have an economic interest in their personal finances and those of their
immediate families. As stated in Regulation 18703.5, u gorr-*ental decision will have a
personal financial effect "if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or
liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing.,,
Steps Four through Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decisions will have a material financial effect on the economic intereJts?
You have conceded that the govemmental decisions involved will materially affect the
Directors' economic interests. Each Director h,as real property that will experirrr"" , change in
value and each Director's personal finances will also bi aifecieo. Whether it is reasonabli
foreseeable that the decisions will have a material effect on each of the other economic interests
does not affect this analysis, as seen in Step Seven, below.
Step Seven: Public Generally
Each of the Directors has a conflict of interest in making govemmental decisions related
to the Project. Notwithstanding a determination that the reason-ab--ly foreseeable financial effect
of a govemmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material, a public official
does not have a disqualifying conflict of interest in the govemmental decision if the official can
establish that the governmental decision will affect the public official's economic interests in a
manner which is indistinguishable from the manner in wtrictr the decision will affect the public
generally. (Regulation 18707.)
You explained that the District will be imposing tru(es or fees on the properties within its
jurisdiction to complete the Project. Th9-re are splcial irles that apply to rates, assessments, and
other similar decisions. The financial effect of a governmental decision on an official,s
economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally in certain instances,
including when the decision is made by the governing board of u *-ut"r, irrguiior, or similar
district to establish or adjust assessments, taxes, fees,-charges, or rates, among other decisions.
The amounts must be applied proportionally on the officiJs' interests as well as at least ten
p:rcent of the property owners or those receiving services from the agency. (Regulation
18707.2(c).)
You have explained that the District will be assessing whether to institute fees or taxes in
addition to other sources of income for the Project. Providel these fees *, ofpii"a o, u
proportional, "across the board" basis and are ilso imposed upon at least ten i"r."nt of the
jurisdiction, the "public generally" exception applies. You have stated that the fees will be
assessed on a proportional basis and will affecribout 50 percent of the properties within the
District, bringing the decisions squarely within this exceition.


File No. A-O9-278
Page No.6
Step Eight: Legally Required participation
while it appears that the "public generally exception" applies, as explained above, there
is another exceptio; that might applv to the Directors as well. Siction szrdt provides a
mechanism through which a decision-making body can avoid paralysis wtren it is impossible to
assemble a quorum of members who are not disquain.a frgm participating in a decision by
Section 87100, the Act's general rule goveming conflicts of interest. sectiln g7l0l provides as
follows:
"Section 87100 does not preven! any public official from making or participating
in the making of a governmental aeciiion to the extent his participation is legally
required for the action or decision to be made. The fact that an official,s vote is
needed to break a tie does not make his participation legally required for purposes
of this section.,'
Regulation 18708 elaborates the process by which Section 87101 is given effect. In brief,
when a decision-making body cannot muster a quorum of members free froir any conflict of
interest, one or more.ofthe disqualified officials - the number to make up a bare quorum - are
selected to take part in the decision at issue, and in any ancillary decisions where the conflict
persists. (see generally, Battersby Adviceletter, No. t-oz-r+r.)
Your current request for advice and our prior letter written to the fifth (of five) Director
(stadum Advice Letter, A-07-ol2) concedes that each Director has a disqual-i-fying conflict of
interest' If, after applying the above "Pullic Generally Exception" a quorum of Directors has
conflicts of interest the "Legaily Required Exception;"n;appry.
With respect to the method to be employed for selecting which disqualified
councilmember is "legally required" to purticipute in a decision, we have advised that a random
means of selection must be used to select only the number of ofhcials n""ii.-1ri6ony Advice
Letter' No' I-08-007') Also, all disqualified iirectors musiparticipate in the selection process.
(See Steele Advice Letter, No. 4-05-071.)
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
\
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division
(hfufit{l^^/h^
HMRjgl
By:

CONCLUSION: The "public generally" and "legally required" exceptions do appear to apply to the
Directors' situations and would allow each to vote, despitca disqualifying confict of interest.

of the first phase.

File No. A-09-278 Page No. 2 You are General Counsel for the Hi-Desert Water District (the "District") and you request advice on behalf of elected Directors Sheldon Hough, Sarann Graham, Roger irlayer, and Dan Munsey (collectively, the "Directors"). The DirectorJwill be participating in decisions related to a proposed construction of a regional wastewater collection, treatment, and disposal system (the "Project") that will be on-going-and accomplished in three phases. The project will serve residential, commercial, and industrial customers and will aim to accomplish the iollowing: l) reduce and/or replace the need for existing sewer septic systems, 2) improvl the collection and treatment of wastewater, and 3) reduce the amount of nitrate contamination affecting the Warren Valley Groundwater Basin. The District will attempt to cover costs through bonds, grants, low-interest loans or other alternatives. There will likely be costs to the public,-however, i-ncluding contributions by property owners with improved real property in the District, future coniection fees, and contributions through existing ratepayers in the District. The rate structure is currently under review. The first phase will involve constructing a wastewater reclamation facility designed for an initial one million gallons per day treatmentiapacity, developing a sewage collection system to^ serve the developed area of Yucca valley, consiructing percolation basins for the treated effluent to return the effluent to the WarrenValley Grouid'water Basin.
